claim_text: However clinical impact is severely limited by the short 2-min 15O half-life17,18,19.

sub_claims.text: 15O PET methodology for measuring brain oxygen utilization uses the short-lived 15O isotope (half-life ~2 min / 123 sec), a property that constrains scan protocols and practical deployment.

evidence.snippet: The introduction of molecular oxygen-15 (T1/2 = 123 sec) into the vascular compartment of the body results in the distribution of the labeled oxygen to the tissues, where it is converted to 0-15-labeled water of metabolism.

The accepted counts were stored in a small laboratory computer, which processed these data including corrections for electronic dead time loss, physical decay (0-15 half-life = 123 sec), and background...
All samples were corrected for radioactive decay (T1/2 = 123 sec) back to the zero time...
The use of short-lived oxygen-15 permits the three sequential scans, each following an administration of H2 15 O, C 15 O, or [ 15 O]oxygen...

sub_claims.paper_value: 123 sec (2 min 3 sec) — the canonical 15O half-life
sub_claims.nuance: mintun1984 is the foundational methods paper for brain 15O PET and repeatedly reports the 123-sec half-life that directly motivates the manuscript's 'short 2-min' characterization. While the paper does not explicitly frame the half-life as a 'clinical-impact limitation' (its purpose is to establish the method), it documents the scan-duration and decay-correction logistics (e.g., 40-sec scans, three sequential 15O administrations, on-site cyclotron/delivery complexity) that make the half-life the practical constraint the manuscript invokes.

phrasings_tried: short lived oxygen-15 PET brain CMRO2 methodology
T1/2 oxygen-15 scan duration decay correction

section_checklist.section: Implementation. The PETT VI system a positron
Calculation of CMRO2 and E requires local CBF

attestation.indirect_attribution_check: mintun1984 is itself the primary methodology citation for brain 15O PET (Raichle, Mintun et al., J Nucl Med 1984); the half-life figure is a physical constant, not indirect attribution.
attestation.out_of_context_check: Manuscript cites mintun1984 alongside vlassenko2015 and baron2012 in a sentence about the short 2-min half-life limiting clinical impact. mintun1984 establishes the half-life and the scan protocols that stem from it; it is an appropriate primary methods citation for the half-life figure even though it does not itself editorialize about 'clinical impact.' Consistent with its role as a foundational methods reference.

sibling_verdicts.excerpt: vlassenko2015 (ingested; see C020): 'Our PET measurements of AG and CMRO 2 are technically demanding and require 15 O inhalation and injection which are not easily available at PET centers' — adjacent support for clinical-availability limitation driven by the short half-life.
